  • Natasha Candeias (Property Progressors) - sales progression specialist
  • Dan Firth (Bid.) - auction industry leader
  • David Mintz (Kerfuffle) - marketing director

Awards designed for independent recognition

The awards programme, launched earlier this year, specifically celebrates excellence among self-employed agents and brokerage businesses. Unlike some industry awards, finalists will be selected against predefined criteria rather than by event organisers.

Chris Webb, awards founder and estate agency consultant, explained: "We've prioritised creating a transparent judging process with professionals who represent different property disciplines. Their combined expertise should provide fair assessments that entrants can trust."

Nominations remain open until 31st July, with both individuals and teams encouraged to apply. The submission process is free, allowing colleagues, clients or the agents themselves to put forward candidates. The organisers have emphasised that the awards aim to highlight genuine merit across the personal agency sector.

For those considering entering, full details about categories, criteria and the nomination process are available on the awards website. With judging now underway, the property industry will watch closely to see which independent agents receive recognition this year.

item.title: ‘Worst may be over’ as mortgage rates fall
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Mortgage rates have seen their most significant monthly decline since October 2024, offering potential relief to borrowers after a prolonged period of elevated costs. While this downward trend suggests some stabilisation in the market, experts caution that economic headwinds continue to influence lenders' pricing strategies.

Recent Mortgage Rate Movements

The latest data shows average fixed-rate mortgage deals falling by between 0.15% and 0.25% across various loan terms, marking the most substantial single-month reduction in nearly two years. This shift follows consecutive base rate holds by the Bank of England and cooling inflation figures, which have allowed lenders to gradually ease their pricing.

Market observers note that while the downward trajectory is encouraging, current rates remain considerably higher than the historic lows seen before 2022. The most competitive two-year fixed rates now typically start around 4.3% for borrowers with 40% equity or deposit, while five-year deals can be found from 3.9% for similar loan-to-value ratios.

Economic Factors Influencing Rates

Several macroeconomic indicators continue to shape mortgage pricing:

  • Inflation has retreated closer to the Bank of England's 2% target, reducing pressure for further base rate hikes
  • Swap rates, which influence fixed mortgage pricing, have shown increased volatility in recent weeks
  • Lender competition has intensified as the property market shows tentative signs of recovery

Prospective borrowers might consider reviewing their options carefully, as some analysts suggest this could represent the beginning of a more sustained period of rate moderation. However, with global economic uncertainties persisting, particularly around energy markets and geopolitical tensions, future rate movements remain difficult to predict with certainty.

Mortgage advisers typically recommend that homeowners explore their remortgaging options several months before their current deal expires, as securing a rate in advance can provide protection against potential future increases. Those considering a house purchase may wish to consult with a whole-of-market broker to identify lenders offering the most competitive terms for their specific circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Mortgage rates have seen their most significant monthly decline since October 2024, offering potential relief to borrowers after a prolonged period of elevated costs. While this downward trend suggests some stabilisation in the market, experts caution that economic headwinds continue to influence lenders' pricing strategies.

Recent Mortgage Rate Movements

The latest data shows average fixed-rate mortgage deals falling by between 0.15% and 0.25% across various loan terms, marking the most substantial single-month reduction in nearly two years. This shift follows consecutive base rate holds by the Bank of England and cooling inflation figures, which have allowed lenders to gradually ease their pricing.

Market observers note that while the downward trajectory is encouraging, current rates remain considerably higher than the historic lows seen before 2022. The most competitive two-year fixed rates now typically start around 4.3% for borrowers with 40% equity or deposit, while five-year deals can be found from 3.9% for similar loan-to-value ratios.

Economic Factors Influencing Rates

Several macroeconomic indicators continue to shape mortgage pricing:

  • Inflation has retreated closer to the Bank of England's 2% target, reducing pressure for further base rate hikes
  • Swap rates, which influence fixed mortgage pricing, have shown increased volatility in recent weeks
  • Lender competition has intensified as the property market shows tentative signs of recovery

Prospective borrowers might consider reviewing their options carefully, as some analysts suggest this could represent the beginning of a more sustained period of rate moderation. However, with global economic uncertainties persisting, particularly around energy markets and geopolitical tensions, future rate movements remain difficult to predict with certainty.

Mortgage advisers typically recommend that homeowners explore their remortgaging options several months before their current deal expires, as securing a rate in advance can provide protection against potential future increases. Those considering a house purchase may wish to consult with a whole-of-market broker to identify lenders offering the most competitive terms for their specific circumstances.

item.title: Buy-to-Let Upgrades with Strong Long-Term Returns
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords looking to maximise their rental yields often focus on high-end upgrades, but the most cost-effective improvements are frequently the practical ones. Whilst luxury finishes may attract tenants initially, it’s the ongoing maintenance issues and energy inefficiencies that can erode long-term returns. Addressing these fundamental concerns could prove more valuable than cosmetic enhancements.

Prioritising Cost-Effective Upgrades

Buy-to-let investors might consider focusing on upgrades that reduce maintenance costs and improve energy efficiency. Common problem areas that could benefit from attention include:

  • Damp and insulation: Recurring damp patches often indicate underlying issues with insulation or ventilation. Properly addressing these can prevent costly repairs down the line.
  • Heating systems: Older boilers and inefficient radiators may lead to higher energy bills and tenant complaints. Modern systems could improve comfort while reducing running costs.
  • Windows and doors: Draughty properties not only increase heating costs but may also deter potential tenants during colder months.

Balancing Appeal and Practicality

Whilst functionality should take priority, landlords shouldn’t overlook the importance of keeping properties competitively modern. Simple, affordable updates could make a significant difference:

  • Neutral décor refreshes can make rooms feel brighter and more spacious without requiring major investment.
  • Updating tired kitchens and bathrooms with mid-range fittings may offer better returns than premium options.
  • Adding practical storage solutions could appeal to tenants looking for functional living spaces.

By focusing on improvements that combine practicality with aesthetic appeal, landlords may find they achieve better long-term returns than with purely cosmetic upgrades. The key lies in identifying which changes will genuinely enhance the rental experience while keeping maintenance costs manageable.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords looking to maximise their rental yields often focus on high-end upgrades, but the most cost-effective improvements are frequently the practical ones. Whilst luxury finishes may attract tenants initially, it’s the ongoing maintenance issues and energy inefficiencies that can erode long-term returns. Addressing these fundamental concerns could prove more valuable than cosmetic enhancements.

Prioritising Cost-Effective Upgrades

Buy-to-let investors might consider focusing on upgrades that reduce maintenance costs and improve energy efficiency. Common problem areas that could benefit from attention include:

  • Damp and insulation: Recurring damp patches often indicate underlying issues with insulation or ventilation. Properly addressing these can prevent costly repairs down the line.
  • Heating systems: Older boilers and inefficient radiators may lead to higher energy bills and tenant complaints. Modern systems could improve comfort while reducing running costs.
  • Windows and doors: Draughty properties not only increase heating costs but may also deter potential tenants during colder months.

Balancing Appeal and Practicality

Whilst functionality should take priority, landlords shouldn’t overlook the importance of keeping properties competitively modern. Simple, affordable updates could make a significant difference:

  • Neutral décor refreshes can make rooms feel brighter and more spacious without requiring major investment.
  • Updating tired kitchens and bathrooms with mid-range fittings may offer better returns than premium options.
  • Adding practical storage solutions could appeal to tenants looking for functional living spaces.

By focusing on improvements that combine practicality with aesthetic appeal, landlords may find they achieve better long-term returns than with purely cosmetic upgrades. The key lies in identifying which changes will genuinely enhance the rental experience while keeping maintenance costs manageable.

item.title: What estate agents should never do to win an instruction – do you agree?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

In the competitive world of property sales, estate agents face ongoing pressure to secure instructions from homeowners. However, one Essex agent has highlighted a common yet counterproductive tactic that some professionals employ: overvaluing properties. Jamie Carr, director and Community Engagement Officer at Accord Sales & Lettings in Romford, has urged his peers to resist this practice, warning that it often leads to prolonged selling times and repeated price reductions.

The pitfalls of overvaluation

Carr, who shared his insights on social media, described inflated valuations as one of the most frequent mistakes he observes in his local market. He believes this trend is widespread across the UK, driven by agents aiming to win instructions by telling sellers what they want to hear. However, Carr emphasised that asking prices not backed by market evidence rarely benefit either the seller or the agent.

With buyers now more informed than ever, thanks to easy access to sold price data, comparable listings, and detailed market insights, overpriced properties are quickly identified. This can result in fewer enquiries, reduced viewings, and longer periods on the market. Ultimately, sellers may be forced to reduce their asking price multiple times, which can diminish their confidence and prolong the selling process.

The case for realistic pricing

Carr advocates for a different approach: pricing properties realistically from the outset. By setting an accurate and evidence-based asking price, sellers are more likely to attract genuine buyers and generate competition. This strategy not only increases the chances of a quicker sale but also helps achieve the strongest possible outcome for the seller.

While honest, evidence-based advice may not win every instruction, Carr argues that it fosters trust and protects an agent’s reputation. Over time, this approach can lead to better long-term results for both agents and their clients. It also ensures that sellers feel supported throughout the process, rather than disappointed by unmet expectations.

Building trust in a competitive market

In a crowded marketplace, estate agents face the challenge of standing out whilst maintaining integrity. Carr’s advice serves as a reminder that transparency and professionalism are key to building lasting relationships with clients. By prioritising accurate valuations and honest communication, agents can position themselves as trusted advisors rather than simply salespeople.

For homeowners, understanding the importance of realistic pricing could help them navigate the selling process more effectively. Seeking out agents who provide evidence-based valuations may lead to smoother transactions and more satisfactory outcomes.

As the property market continues to evolve, the role of estate agents remains crucial. By avoiding shortcuts like overvaluation and focusing on long-term success, professionals can ensure they deliver value to their clients whilst maintaining their own credibility in an increasingly competitive industry.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

In the competitive world of property sales, estate agents face ongoing pressure to secure instructions from homeowners. However, one Essex agent has highlighted a common yet counterproductive tactic that some professionals employ: overvaluing properties. Jamie Carr, director and Community Engagement Officer at Accord Sales & Lettings in Romford, has urged his peers to resist this practice, warning that it often leads to prolonged selling times and repeated price reductions.

The pitfalls of overvaluation

Carr, who shared his insights on social media, described inflated valuations as one of the most frequent mistakes he observes in his local market. He believes this trend is widespread across the UK, driven by agents aiming to win instructions by telling sellers what they want to hear. However, Carr emphasised that asking prices not backed by market evidence rarely benefit either the seller or the agent.

With buyers now more informed than ever, thanks to easy access to sold price data, comparable listings, and detailed market insights, overpriced properties are quickly identified. This can result in fewer enquiries, reduced viewings, and longer periods on the market. Ultimately, sellers may be forced to reduce their asking price multiple times, which can diminish their confidence and prolong the selling process.

The case for realistic pricing

Carr advocates for a different approach: pricing properties realistically from the outset. By setting an accurate and evidence-based asking price, sellers are more likely to attract genuine buyers and generate competition. This strategy not only increases the chances of a quicker sale but also helps achieve the strongest possible outcome for the seller.

While honest, evidence-based advice may not win every instruction, Carr argues that it fosters trust and protects an agent’s reputation. Over time, this approach can lead to better long-term results for both agents and their clients. It also ensures that sellers feel supported throughout the process, rather than disappointed by unmet expectations.

Building trust in a competitive market

In a crowded marketplace, estate agents face the challenge of standing out whilst maintaining integrity. Carr’s advice serves as a reminder that transparency and professionalism are key to building lasting relationships with clients. By prioritising accurate valuations and honest communication, agents can position themselves as trusted advisors rather than simply salespeople.

For homeowners, understanding the importance of realistic pricing could help them navigate the selling process more effectively. Seeking out agents who provide evidence-based valuations may lead to smoother transactions and more satisfactory outcomes.

As the property market continues to evolve, the role of estate agents remains crucial. By avoiding shortcuts like overvaluation and focusing on long-term success, professionals can ensure they deliver value to their clients whilst maintaining their own credibility in an increasingly competitive industry.

item.title: Beyond Marbella: Where Costa del Sol investors are finding value in 2026
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

For decades, Marbella has been the go-to destination for UK property investors eyeing the Costa del Sol. Its glamorous reputation, bustling lifestyle, and prime location have made it a hotspot for those seeking both luxury and potential returns. However, as we approach 2026, the investment landscape in this iconic Spanish region is shifting. With Marbella’s property market becoming increasingly competitive and supply tightening, savvy investors are now exploring alternative areas along the Costa del Sol that offer better value and growth potential.

Why Marbella May No Longer Be the Default Choice

Marbella’s prime locations have seen significant price increases in recent years, driven by high demand and limited availability. While this has been lucrative for existing property owners, it has made entry points for new investors more challenging. The combination of soaring prices and a competitive market means that opportunities for substantial returns are becoming harder to find. For those looking to invest in the Costa del Sol, it could be worth considering other towns and regions that offer similar appeal but with more attractive price tags and growth prospects.

Emerging Hotspots Along the Costa del Sol

Several areas along the Costa del Sol are gaining attention from property investors seeking value beyond Marbella. Estepona, for example, has been dubbed the “new Marbella” due to its charming old town, modern amenities, and more affordable property prices. Its proximity to Marbella, coupled with ongoing infrastructure improvements, makes it an appealing option for those looking to capitalise on the region’s popularity without the premium price tag.

Further east, Nerja is another destination worth considering. Known for its stunning beaches and laid-back atmosphere, Nerja offers a more authentic Spanish experience compared to the bustling tourist hubs. Property prices here remain relatively modest, and the town’s growing reputation as a cultural and culinary destination could drive future demand.

For investors seeking a blend of affordability and convenience, Mijas Costa presents a compelling case. Situated between Malaga and Marbella, this area offers easy access to major transport links whilst retaining a quieter, family-friendly vibe. Its mix of traditional charm and modern developments makes it an attractive choice for both holiday lets and long-term rentals.

Factors to Consider When Investing Beyond Marbella

Whilst exploring alternative locations, investors might wish to assess several key factors to ensure a sound investment. Proximity to amenities, transport links, and popular attractions can significantly influence a property’s rental potential and resale value. Additionally, understanding local market trends, such as demand for holiday homes versus permanent residences, can help tailor investment strategies to maximise returns.

It’s also important to consider the legal and financial aspects of buying property in Spain, including taxes, fees, and any regulatory changes that may impact the market. Seeking advice from local property experts or financial advisors could provide valuable insights and help navigate the complexities of overseas investment.

As the Costa del Sol continues to evolve, opportunities for property investors are expanding beyond its most famous town. By exploring emerging hotspots and carefully evaluating market conditions, UK investors can uncover hidden gems that offer both value and potential for growth in the years to come.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

For decades, Marbella has been the go-to destination for UK property investors eyeing the Costa del Sol. Its glamorous reputation, bustling lifestyle, and prime location have made it a hotspot for those seeking both luxury and potential returns. However, as we approach 2026, the investment landscape in this iconic Spanish region is shifting. With Marbella’s property market becoming increasingly competitive and supply tightening, savvy investors are now exploring alternative areas along the Costa del Sol that offer better value and growth potential.

Why Marbella May No Longer Be the Default Choice

Marbella’s prime locations have seen significant price increases in recent years, driven by high demand and limited availability. While this has been lucrative for existing property owners, it has made entry points for new investors more challenging. The combination of soaring prices and a competitive market means that opportunities for substantial returns are becoming harder to find. For those looking to invest in the Costa del Sol, it could be worth considering other towns and regions that offer similar appeal but with more attractive price tags and growth prospects.

Emerging Hotspots Along the Costa del Sol

Several areas along the Costa del Sol are gaining attention from property investors seeking value beyond Marbella. Estepona, for example, has been dubbed the “new Marbella” due to its charming old town, modern amenities, and more affordable property prices. Its proximity to Marbella, coupled with ongoing infrastructure improvements, makes it an appealing option for those looking to capitalise on the region’s popularity without the premium price tag.

Further east, Nerja is another destination worth considering. Known for its stunning beaches and laid-back atmosphere, Nerja offers a more authentic Spanish experience compared to the bustling tourist hubs. Property prices here remain relatively modest, and the town’s growing reputation as a cultural and culinary destination could drive future demand.

For investors seeking a blend of affordability and convenience, Mijas Costa presents a compelling case. Situated between Malaga and Marbella, this area offers easy access to major transport links whilst retaining a quieter, family-friendly vibe. Its mix of traditional charm and modern developments makes it an attractive choice for both holiday lets and long-term rentals.

Factors to Consider When Investing Beyond Marbella

Whilst exploring alternative locations, investors might wish to assess several key factors to ensure a sound investment. Proximity to amenities, transport links, and popular attractions can significantly influence a property’s rental potential and resale value. Additionally, understanding local market trends, such as demand for holiday homes versus permanent residences, can help tailor investment strategies to maximise returns.

It’s also important to consider the legal and financial aspects of buying property in Spain, including taxes, fees, and any regulatory changes that may impact the market. Seeking advice from local property experts or financial advisors could provide valuable insights and help navigate the complexities of overseas investment.

As the Costa del Sol continues to evolve, opportunities for property investors are expanding beyond its most famous town. By exploring emerging hotspots and carefully evaluating market conditions, UK investors can uncover hidden gems that offer both value and potential for growth in the years to come.

item.title: UK HMO hotspots revealed
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research has highlighted the UK’s leading hotspots for houses in multiple occupation (HMOs), with university cities Oxford and Cambridge dominating the rankings outside of London. The findings come as demand for shared accommodation remains strong despite recent regulatory changes affecting the sector.

Oxford leads regional HMO markets

Analysis by Property Buyers Today reveals Oxford contains 2,664 HMOs of various sizes, representing 4.52% of the city’s total housing stock – the highest proportion outside the capital. The historic university town’s combination of student populations and high rental demand has created ideal conditions for HMO investments.

Cambridge follows closely behind with 3.68% of its properties registered as HMOs. Both cities benefit from:

  • Strong tenant demand from student and young professional markets
  • High rental yields compared to average property values
  • Concentrated populations in need of affordable accommodation

Regulatory changes fail to dampen market

The research suggests the HMO sector continues to demonstrate resilience despite recent licensing changes and tighter regulations. Many local authorities have introduced additional licensing schemes, requiring more properties to meet safety and management standards.

For landlords considering HMO investments, it could be worth:

  • Researching local licensing requirements thoroughly
  • Factoring in potential higher management costs
  • Seeking specialist mortgage advice for multi-let properties

While the upfront costs and compliance requirements have increased, the sustained demand in key locations means HMOs remain an attractive proposition for many property investors. Those entering the market may wish to focus on areas with proven rental demand and clear tenant demographics.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research has highlighted the UK’s leading hotspots for houses in multiple occupation (HMOs), with university cities Oxford and Cambridge dominating the rankings outside of London. The findings come as demand for shared accommodation remains strong despite recent regulatory changes affecting the sector.

Oxford leads regional HMO markets

Analysis by Property Buyers Today reveals Oxford contains 2,664 HMOs of various sizes, representing 4.52% of the city’s total housing stock – the highest proportion outside the capital. The historic university town’s combination of student populations and high rental demand has created ideal conditions for HMO investments.

Cambridge follows closely behind with 3.68% of its properties registered as HMOs. Both cities benefit from:

  • Strong tenant demand from student and young professional markets
  • High rental yields compared to average property values
  • Concentrated populations in need of affordable accommodation

Regulatory changes fail to dampen market

The research suggests the HMO sector continues to demonstrate resilience despite recent licensing changes and tighter regulations. Many local authorities have introduced additional licensing schemes, requiring more properties to meet safety and management standards.

For landlords considering HMO investments, it could be worth:

  • Researching local licensing requirements thoroughly
  • Factoring in potential higher management costs
  • Seeking specialist mortgage advice for multi-let properties

While the upfront costs and compliance requirements have increased, the sustained demand in key locations means HMOs remain an attractive proposition for many property investors. Those entering the market may wish to focus on areas with proven rental demand and clear tenant demographics.

item.title: Worries that mortgage fraud not being identified
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Recent data obtained through a Freedom of Information (FOI) request has revealed that only one mortgage fraud investigation was carried out by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) in 2025. This has sparked concerns among industry experts about the effectiveness of fraud detection and enforcement within the UK mortgage sector.

Concerns Over Fraud Detection

The low number of investigations has raised questions about whether mortgage fraud is being adequately identified and addressed. Fraudulent activities in the mortgage market can include misrepresentation of income, falsification of documents, or the use of false identities. Such practices not only undermine the integrity of the financial system but can also lead to significant financial losses for lenders and borrowers alike.

Industry professionals have highlighted the importance of robust detection mechanisms to prevent fraud from going unnoticed. With the complexity of mortgage applications and the increasing use of digital platforms, fraudsters may find new ways to exploit vulnerabilities. Experts suggest that lenders and regulators need to work closely together to enhance monitoring and reporting systems.

The Role of Technology in Fraud Prevention

Advancements in technology could play a crucial role in identifying and preventing mortgage fraud. Tools such as artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning can analyse large volumes of data to detect unusual patterns or inconsistencies in applications. These technologies may help lenders flag potential cases of fraud more efficiently, reducing the risk of undetected criminal activity.

Additionally, the use of blockchain technology has been proposed as a way to improve transparency and security in the mortgage process. By creating an immutable record of transactions and documents, blockchain could make it more difficult for fraudsters to manipulate information. However, the adoption of such technologies would require significant investment and collaboration across the industry.

Regulatory Challenges

Despite the potential benefits of technology, regulatory challenges remain. The FCA has a broad remit to oversee financial markets and protect consumers, but limited resources may restrict its ability to investigate every suspected case of fraud. Some experts argue that greater funding and staffing for regulatory bodies could improve enforcement efforts.

Moreover, borrowers and lenders alike have a role to play in combating fraud. Homeowners may wish to ensure that their personal information is secure and to report any suspicious activity to their lender or the authorities. Lenders, on the other hand, might consider implementing stricter verification processes and educating their staff about the latest fraud tactics.

While the FCA’s efforts to investigate mortgage fraud are commendable, the low number of cases identified in 2025 has highlighted the need for continued vigilance and innovation in fraud prevention. By leveraging technology, strengthening regulatory frameworks, and fostering collaboration across the industry, stakeholders can work towards a more secure and transparent mortgage market.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Recent data obtained through a Freedom of Information (FOI) request has revealed that only one mortgage fraud investigation was carried out by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) in 2025. This has sparked concerns among industry experts about the effectiveness of fraud detection and enforcement within the UK mortgage sector.

Concerns Over Fraud Detection

The low number of investigations has raised questions about whether mortgage fraud is being adequately identified and addressed. Fraudulent activities in the mortgage market can include misrepresentation of income, falsification of documents, or the use of false identities. Such practices not only undermine the integrity of the financial system but can also lead to significant financial losses for lenders and borrowers alike.

Industry professionals have highlighted the importance of robust detection mechanisms to prevent fraud from going unnoticed. With the complexity of mortgage applications and the increasing use of digital platforms, fraudsters may find new ways to exploit vulnerabilities. Experts suggest that lenders and regulators need to work closely together to enhance monitoring and reporting systems.

The Role of Technology in Fraud Prevention

Advancements in technology could play a crucial role in identifying and preventing mortgage fraud. Tools such as artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning can analyse large volumes of data to detect unusual patterns or inconsistencies in applications. These technologies may help lenders flag potential cases of fraud more efficiently, reducing the risk of undetected criminal activity.

Additionally, the use of blockchain technology has been proposed as a way to improve transparency and security in the mortgage process. By creating an immutable record of transactions and documents, blockchain could make it more difficult for fraudsters to manipulate information. However, the adoption of such technologies would require significant investment and collaboration across the industry.

Regulatory Challenges

Despite the potential benefits of technology, regulatory challenges remain. The FCA has a broad remit to oversee financial markets and protect consumers, but limited resources may restrict its ability to investigate every suspected case of fraud. Some experts argue that greater funding and staffing for regulatory bodies could improve enforcement efforts.

Moreover, borrowers and lenders alike have a role to play in combating fraud. Homeowners may wish to ensure that their personal information is secure and to report any suspicious activity to their lender or the authorities. Lenders, on the other hand, might consider implementing stricter verification processes and educating their staff about the latest fraud tactics.

While the FCA’s efforts to investigate mortgage fraud are commendable, the low number of cases identified in 2025 has highlighted the need for continued vigilance and innovation in fraud prevention. By leveraging technology, strengthening regulatory frameworks, and fostering collaboration across the industry, stakeholders can work towards a more secure and transparent mortgage market.
